title: Reconnaissance — The Foundation of Every Engagement

sections.title: Why Reconnaissance Matters
sections.content:
Reconnaissance is the most critical phase of penetration testing. Without thorough recon, you're attacking blind — wasting time on hardened targets while missing easy wins. Professional pentesters spend 60-70% of their time on recon because the quality of your intelligence directly determines the success of exploitation.

Think of it like a burglar casing a building: they don't just run at the front door. They observe schedules, identify cameras, find unlocked windows, and map escape routes. In pentesting, recon reveals the attack surface — every exposed service, every forgotten subdomain, every leaked credential is a potential entry point.

**Real-world tie-in:** The 2019 Capital One breach (by Paige Thompson) started with recon. She discovered a misconfigured WAF on a CloudFront distribution, then used the metadata endpoint to harvest IAM credentials. No exploit needed — just thorough recon of cloud infrastructure. The breach affected 100+ million credit card applications.

**Why this matters in your career:** The best pentesters are the best researchers. Learning recon teaches you attention to detail, systematic thinking, and how to connect seemingly unrelated information — skills that transfer to defense, forensics, and engineering roles equally.

sections.title: Passive vs Active Reconnaissance
sections.content:
**Passive recon** gathers information without directly touching the target. The target has zero visibility into your activities. Sources include: DNS records, certificate transparency logs, WHOIS, social media, job postings, GitHub repos, Shodan/Censys, and cached pages.

**Active recon** directly interacts with the target — port scans, banner grabs, directory brute-force. The target CAN detect this. IDS/IPS may alert, firewalls may block, and logs will record your activity.

**When to use which:**
- Always start passive — it's free intelligence with zero risk
- Move to active only after exhausting passive sources
- During red team engagements, minimize active recon to avoid detection
- In standard pentests, active recon is expected and acceptable

**Worked example — Passive recon of a target domain:**
```
# Certificate Transparency logs (crt.sh) - never touches target
$ curl -s 'https://crt.sh/?q=example.com&output=json' | jq -r '.[].name_value' | sort -u
admin.example.com
dev.example.com
mail.example.com
vpn.example.com

# theHarvester - passive sources
$ theHarvester -d example.com -b google,linkedin,bing
[*] Hosts found: 15
[*] Emails found: 34
[*] IPs found: 8

# DNS record enumeration (passive)
$ dig +short TXT example.com
"v=spf1 include:_spf.google.com ~all"
$ dig +short MX example.com
10 mail.example.com.
```

**Gotcha:** Just because you used a passive tool doesn't mean you're anonymous. A reverse DNS lookup against YOUR DNS resolver can identify you. Use public resolvers (8.8.8.8) or better, your own OSINT infrastructure.

sections.title: Reconnaissance Methodology
sections.content:
Follow this systematic approach:

1. **Scope Definition** — What are you allowed to test? IP ranges, domains, out-of-scope systems
2. **Passive OSINT** — Subdomains (crt.sh, subfinder), emails (theHarvester), leaked creds (dehashed), tech stack (Wappalyzer)
3. **DNS Enumeration** — Zone transfers, subdomain brute-force, record types (MX, TXT, SPF)
4. **Network Mapping** — Port scanning (nmap), service detection, OS fingerprinting
5. **Web Recon** — Directory discovery, virtual hosts, technology fingerprinting, WAF detection
6. **Documentation** — Record everything. A missed finding during recon means a missed vulnerability later.

**Worked example — Full recon pipeline against a target IP range:**
```
# Step 1: Quick live host discovery
$ nmap -sn 10.10.10.0/24 | grep -E "Nmap scan|Host is up"
Nmap scan report for 10.10.10.1
Host is up (0.001s latency).
Nmap scan report for 10.10.10.50
Host is up (0.002s latency).

# Step 2: Service scan on live hosts
$ nmap -sV -sC -p- 10.10.10.50 -oA recon/50_full
# Output saved to recon/50_full.nmap, .gnmap, .xml

# Step 3: Extract web servers
$ grep -E "http|https|443|8080|8443" recon/50_full.nmap
80/tcp   open  http    Apache httpd 2.4.41
443/tcp  open  https   Apache httpd 2.4.41
8080/tcp open  http    nginx 1.18.0

# Step 4: Technology identification
$ whatweb http://10.10.10.50
http://10.10.10.50 [200 OK] Apache[2.4.41], PHP[7.4.3], HTML5, HTTPServer[Ubuntu Linux], JQuery
```

The output of recon feeds directly into the next phases. Every open port is a potential vulnerability. Every subdomain is a potential target. Every technology identified narrows your exploit search.

sections.title: Subdomain Enumeration — Practical Deep-Dive
sections.content:
Subdomain enumeration finds targets the client may have forgotten. Internal apps, staging servers, admin panels — these are often exposed with less security than production.

**Three approaches, all complementary:**

**1. Passive (certificate transparency):**
```bash
$ subfinder -d example.com -all -o subdomains_passive.txt
[INF] Found 247 subdomains
$ head -5 subdomains_passive.txt
admin.example.com
api.example.com
staging.example.com
jenkins.example.com
jira.example.com
```

**2. DNS brute-force (active):**
Use a large wordlist like `subdomains-top1million-5000.txt` from SecLists.
```bash
$ gobuster dns -d example.com -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/DNS/subdomains-top1million-110000.txt -t 100
Found: dev.example.com
Found: internal.example.com
Found: db.example.com
Found: sso.example.com
```

**3. Resolver verification:**
Not all found subdomains are alive. Filter with httpx.
```bash
$ httpx -l subdomains_passive.txt -status-code -title -o live_subdomains.txt
https://admin.example.com [200] [Admin Dashboard]
http://api.example.com [502] [Bad Gateway]
http://staging.example.com [401] [Authorization Required]
http://jenkins.example.com [200] [Jenkins]  # <-- interesting
```

**Gotcha:** DNS brute-force can take hours without a fast resolver. Use `dnsx` with a resolver list (`dnsx -l domains.txt -r resolvers.txt`) and always verify with httpx — DNS doesn't mean HTTP is accessible.

**Real-world tie-in:** Bug bounty hunters routinely find critical bugs on `staging.example.com` or `admin.example.com` — subdomains the client forgot about. These often run older software with known vulnerabilities.

sections.title: Advanced DNS Reconnaissance
sections.content:
DNS records reveal far more than just IP addresses. Each record type tells you something:

| Record | What it reveals | Security relevance |
|--------|----------------|-------------------|
| SOA | Name server, admin email | May leak internal admin domain |
| MX | Mail servers | Target for social engineering |
| TXT | SPF, DKIM, DMARC | Email security posture (or lack thereof) |
| SPF | Allowed mail senders | Can you spoof email? |
| CNAME | Service aliases | Reveals cloud services (AWS, GCP, Cloudflare) |
| NS | Name servers | Usually hardened, but zone transfer check |

**Worked example — zone transfer (rare but gold when it works):**
```bash
$ dig +short NS example.com
ns1.example.com
ns2.example.com

# Try zone transfer (almost always fails, but one attempt costs nothing)
$ dig axfr @ns1.example.com example.com
; Transfer failed.

# Check DNSsec status (might leak zone contents)
$ dig +short DNSKEY example.com | wc -l
4  # DNSSEC enabled

# DMARC record - determines email spoofing risk
$ dig +short TXT _dmarc.example.com
"v=DMARC1; p=reject; rua=mailto:reports@example.com"  # p=reject is good, they catch spoofing
```

**Gotcha:** Just because a zone transfer fails doesn't mean you can't enumerate DNS records. Use `dnsrecon` to try multiple record types and brute-force methods.

**Real-world tie-in:** The 2020 Twitter hack used DNS recon to map internal services. While the initial access was via social engineering (phone spear-phishing), DNS recon helped attackers understand Twitter's internal infrastructure and plan lateral movement.

sections.title: Human OSINT — Employees, Credentials, Social Engineering
sections.content:
Technology is only part of the attack surface. Every employee is a potential entry point through social engineering, and leaked credentials are common initial access vectors.

**Employee OSINT:**
```bash
# Find email addresses
$ theHarvester -d example.com -b linkedin
[*] Users found: 28
[*] Total emails: 34
# Format: jdoe@example.com, msmith@example.com

# Check password breaches (requires breached email list or API)
# dehashed.com, haveibeenpwned.com for individual checks
# Always respect scope! Only test accounts you're authorized to target.

# GitHub OSINT — developers leak credentials constantly
$ git clone https://github.com/example/example-repo.git
$ grep -r "password\|secret\|api_key\|token" --include="*.{py,js,env,yml,yaml,conf}" .
config.py: DB_PASSWORD = "Passw0rd!"  # <-- oops
.env.example: SLACK_TOKEN=  # <-- token line left in, real value in .env
.git/config: url = https://github.com/example/internal-repo.git  # <-- internal repos
```

**Technology stack from job postings:**
Companies hiring for specific roles reveal their tech stack:
- "Senior AWS Security Engineer" → they're on AWS
- "Kubernetes Admin" → containerized infrastructure
- "Elasticsearch Administrator" → likely using the ELK stack

**Social engineering recon:**
- Employee names → email format → phishing targets
- Org chart → executives for whaling attacks
- IT support team → phone numbers for vishing (voice phishing)
- Office locations → physical access assessments

**Gotcha:** Password reuse is the pentester's best friend. An employee's leaked LinkedIn password may also work on the corporate VPN. But be extremely careful with credential testing — lockouts can cause real business impact. Coordinate with the client on password policies.

**Real-world tie-in:** The 2020 SolarWinds attack (by Cozy Bear/APT29) used extensive human OSINT. Attackers researched SolarWinds employees on LinkedIn to identify targets, crafted personalized phishing lures, and gained initial access via a help desk impersonation call to reset a VPN password.

sections.title: Choosing the Right Tool for Each Recon Task
sections.content:
**For subdomain discovery:**
- Start with subfinder/amass (passive, fast)
- Then gobuster/ffuf DNS (active brute-force)
- Verify with httpx (which are alive?)

**For port scanning:**
- Quick scan: rustscan (all ports in seconds) → nmap (service detection on open ports)
- Stealth scan: nmap -sS -T2 (slower but less detectable)
- Full assessment: nmap -sV -sC -p- (comprehensive but noisy)

**For web recon:**
- Technology: whatweb, wappalyzer
- Directories: feroxbuster, gobuster
- WAF: wafw00f (know what you're up against)

**Worked example — Multi-tool recon decision tree:**
```
# Scenario: Standard pentest, in-scope: example.com, no time constraints
$ subfinder -d example.com -all | tee subs.txt && httpx -l subs.txt -o live.txt
$ nmap -sV -sC -iL live_ips.txt -oA full_scan
# 80,443 open → deep web recon
$ whatweb https://example.com
$ feroxbuster -u https://example.com -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirb/common.txt

# Scenario: Red team, any scan triggers SOC, 4-hour window
# Passive only:
$ theHarvester -d example.com -b all
$ curl -s 'https://crt.sh/?q=%.example.com&output=json'
# NO port scans — they alert. Use Shodan/Censys instead.
$ curl -s 'https://internetdb.shodan.io/192.0.2.0'
```

**Key principle:** Use the least noisy tool that gets the job done. In a red team engagement, a single aggressive nmap scan can burn your IP. In a standard pentest, speed matters more than stealth.

**Gotcha:** Not all tools need to be run from a single machine. In a red team scenario, distribute recon across multiple VPS servers (DigitalOcean droplet for passive, AWS EC2 for active) to avoid a single point of detection. Use `proxychains` or a SOCKS proxy when the target has geo-blocking.

sections.title: Documentation & Information Management
sections.content:
A proper pentest produces thousands of data points during recon. Without organization, you'll lose findings and miss exploitation opportunities.

**What to document:**
- All discovered subdomains with HTTP titles and tech stacks
- All open ports per host with service versions
- Any credentials found (with source)
- Interesting files (configs, backups, exposed admin panels)
- Potential attack vectors for later phases

**Worked example — ReCon documentation structure:**
```
recon/
├── targets.txt                  # All in-scope IPs and domains
├── passive/
│   ├── subfinder.txt            # Raw subdomains
│   ├── crtsh.json               # Certificate data
│   └── theharvester.txt         # Emails/domains
├── active/
│   ├── live_hosts.txt           # Verified alive hosts
│   ├── nmap/
│   │   ├── quick_scan.xml       # Machine-readable
│   │   ├── full_scan.nmap       # Human-readable
│   │   └── services.txt         # Extracted service list
│   └── web/
│       ├── screenshots/         # Visual reference
│       └── interesting_paths.txt# Found directories
└── notes.md                     # Free-form observations
```

**Tools for documentation:**
- `Screenshots` — `cutycapt` or `eyewitness` for mass web screenshotting
- `Note-taking` — KeepNote, CherryTree, or simple markdown
- `Collaboration` — Dradis or Faraday for team pentests
- `Flow` — Simple text files + structured naming work best on-site

**Gotcha:** Document AS YOU GO. Trying to reconstruct findings from memory at the end of a 12-hour test day leads to missed details. Set a 5-minute reminder to update notes after every significant discovery. Screenshot everything — you'll thank yourself when writing the report 2 weeks later.

**Real-world tie-in:** In many successful breach post-mortems (Target 2013, Marriott 2018), the attackers documented extensively. The Target attackers maintained detailed network maps that allowed them to navigate from the HVAC vendor portal to the POS card data systems. Good documentation means the difference between "we found X" and "we found X and exploited it to reach Y through Z."

sections.title: Reconnaissance at Scale — Building Automation Pipelines
sections.content:
Professional engagements involve more than one target. When you have multiple IP ranges, hundreds of domains, and strict time constraints, manual recon doesn't scale. A well-architected automation pipeline is essential.

**Worked example — Bash recon pipeline (single-file automation):**
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# full_recon.sh — Automated recon pipeline
# Usage: ./full_recon.sh target.com

TARGET=$1
DIR="recon/$TARGET"
mkdir -p "$DIR"

echo "[+] Starting recon on $TARGET at $(date)"

# Phase 1: Passive subdomain discovery
echo "[*] Phase 1: Passive subdomain enumeration..."
subfinder -d "$TARGET" -all -o "$DIR/subdomains_passive.txt" 2>/dev/null

# Phase 2: Active subdomain brute-force
echo "[*] Phase 2: Active subdomain brute-force..."
gobuster dns -d "$TARGET" -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/DNS/subdomains-top1million-110000.txt \n  -t 100 -o "$DIR/subdomains_active.txt" 2>/dev/null

# Phase 3: Filter live hosts
echo "[*] Phase 3: Filtering live hosts..."
cat "$DIR/subdomains_passive.txt" "$DIR/subdomains_active.txt" | sort -u > "$DIR/all_subs.txt"
httpx -l "$DIR/all_subs.txt" -status-code -title -tech-detect -o "$DIR/live_subs.txt" 2>/dev/null

# Phase 4: Port scan live hosts
echo "[*] Phase 4: Port scanning..."
cut -d' ' -f1 "$DIR/live_subs.txt" | sort -u | while read ip; do
  nmap -sV -sC -p- --min-rate=5000 "$ip" -oA "$DIR/nmap_$ip" 2>/dev/null
done

# Phase 5: Web screenshots
echo "[*] Phase 5: Taking screenshots..."
audit awefile_$TARGET "$DIR/live_subs.txt" 2>/dev/null || \n  echo "[!] EyeWitness not installed, skipping screenshots"

echo "[+] Recon complete for $TARGET at $(date)"
echo "[+] Results in $DIR"
```

**Python automation with asyncio (faster, more control):**
```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Parallel recon pipeline with asyncio.""
import asyncio
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path

DOMAINS = ["example.com", "example.org", "corp.com"]

async def run_tool(cmd, log_file):
    """Run a command and log output asynchronously.""
    proc = await asyncio.create_subprocess_shell(
        cmd, stdout=subprocess.PIPE, stderr=subprocess.PIPE
    )
    stdout, stderr = await proc.communicate()
    Path(log_file).write_bytes(stdout)
    return proc.returncode

async def pipeline():
    tasks = []
    for domain in DOMAINS:
        tasks.append(run_tool(
            f"subfinder -d {domain} -all -o recon/{domain}_subs.txt",
            f"logs/{domain}_subfinder.log"
        ))
    await asyncio.gather(*tasks)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(pipeline())
```

**Intelligence integration — adding Shodan to your pipeline:**
```bash
# Query Shodan for all hosts in your target's ASN
# First find the ASN from a known IP:
$ whois -h whois.radb.net -- "-i origin 10.10.0.1" | grep -E "^origin:"
AS12345

# Then query Shodan for ALL hosts in that ASN
$ shodan search "asn:AS12345" --fields ip_str,port,org,hostnames --limit 1000 \n  -o shodan_asn12345.csv
# Now you have a targeted list from the attacker's perspective

# Integrate into your pipeline:
$ shodan search "asn:AS12345 port:443 http.title:'admin'"
# Finds admin panels on the target's network — even those not DNS-listed
```

**Parallel processing with GNU parallel:**
```bash
# Scan 100 hosts in parallel (not sequential!)
$ cat targets.txt | parallel -j 10 "nmap -sV -p80,443,22 -oG results/{}.txt {}"
# -j 10 runs 10 nmaps simultaneously 
# This completes in 5 minutes instead of 50 minutes

# Smarter throttling with --load
$ cat urls.txt | parallel -j 0 --load 80% "httpx -u {} -silent -status-code" > live_hosts.txt
# --load 80% keeps CPU under 80% utilization
```

**Gotcha:** Automation pipelines are POWERFUL but can get you banned. Mass scanning without rate limiting is detected as a DDoS attack. Always: 1) Add random delays between requests (jitter), 2) Respect robots.txt for passive tools, 3) Use random User-Agent strings, 4) Don't scan entire IP ranges in one burst — spread it across hours. Also, automating exploitation is NEVER okay without explicit written permission from the target.

**Real-world tie-in:** The Bugcrowd platform processes thousands of submissions daily — successful bug bounty hunters use automated recon pipelines to find targets first. The fastest hunters run continuous monitoring pipelines that alert them (via Slack/Discord webhooks) the moment a new subdomain, open port, or technology change is detected on their target scope. First to find often means first to exploit.

**Key tools for automation:**
- `GNU parallel` — Parallelize ANY command across multiple inputs
- `tmux` — Keep recon running in background sessions
- `screen` — Alternative background session manager
- `cron` — Schedule daily/weekly re-scans for changed targets
- `Slack webhooks` — Real-time notifications for new findings

sections.title: Cloud and Mobile Reconnaissance
sections.content:
Modern targets aren't just web servers and IP ranges. Cloud infrastructure and mobile applications represent vast, often overlooked attack surfaces.

**Cloud reconnaissance — AWS:**
```bash
# S3 bucket discovery from domain names
$ bucket-stream
# Or manually:
$ curl -s https://target-backups.s3.amazonaws.com/ -I
HTTP/1.1 200 OK  # Public bucket!
$ aws s3 ls s3://target-backups --no-sign-request
# If this works, you have read access to their bucket

# Generate bucket names from common patterns:
for prefix in target corp backup dev staging prod; do
  curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code} %{url_effective}\
" \n    "https://$prefix.s3.amazonaws.com/"
done
# Check: target-prod, corp-backups, staging-target, etc.

# Cloud IP ranges (for correlating with your nmap results)
$ curl -s https://ip-ranges.amazonaws.com/ip-ranges.json | jq '.prefixes[] | select(.service=="EC2") | .ip_prefix'
# Cross-reference with your target IPs to identify cloud-hosted services

# Azure cloud reconnaissance
$ curl -s https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OWASP/Amass/master/examples/config.ini | grep -i azure
$ curl -s https://targetstorage.blob.core.windows.net/?restype=container&comp=list
# Returns XML listing if container is public

# GCP cloud reconnaissance
$ curl -s https://storage.googleapis.com/target-bucket/
$ gsutil ls gs://target-bucket/ 2>/dev/null
```

**Mobile application reconnaissance — APK analysis:**
```bash
# Step 1: Download the APK
$ wget https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.target.app -O target.apk
# Or use apkeep for cleaner downloads
$ apkeep -a com.target.app .

# Step 2: Decompile with jadx
$ jadx target.apk -d jadx_output/
# This produces readable Java source code from the APK

# Step 3: Search for sensitive info
$ grep -r "https://" jadx_output/ | grep -v "android\|google\|facebook"
https://api.target.com/v3/  # <-- Found the API endpoint!
https://staging-api.target.com/  # <-- Staging endpoint discovered!

$ grep -r "apiKey\|api_key\|secret\|password\|token" jadx_output/ --include="*.java"
# Often strings.xml or BuildConfig contains API keys

# Step 4: Check AndroidManifest for exported activities
$ grep -A 3 "exported="true"" jadx_output/AndroidManifest.xml
# Exported activities can be launched without authentication
# "android:exported="true"" on an activity without proper permissions = vuln
```

**iOS application reconnaissance — IPA analysis:**
```bash
# Step 1: Get the IPA (from a jailbroken device or 3rd-party store)
# Step 2: Unzip (IPA is just a zip)
$ unzip target.ipa -d ipa_output/

# Step 3: Check the Info.plist for URLs and schemes
$ grep -r "https\|http\|api" ipa_output/Payload/*.app/Info.plist

# Step 4: Search binary for hardcoded URLs
$ strings ipa_output/Payload/*.app/target | grep -E "https?://" | sort -u

# Step 5: Check for insecure data storage
$ grep -r "NSUserDefaults\|Keychain\|CoreData" ipa_output/ --include="*.swift" --include="*.m"
```

**Gotcha:** Cloud recon often reveals staging/dev environments that are LESS secured than production but contain the SAME codebase and database schemas. Finding "staging.target.com" often means you can test exploits there without risk to production — and the findings often apply to production too. For mobile APK analysis, even obfuscated apps with ProGuard/DexGuard leave hardcoded strings in resources (strings.xml) that can't be obfuscated. iOS IPA files are harder to obtain than Android APKs (you need a jailbroken device or Apple Developer account), but the information density is similar once you have them.

**Real-world tie-in:** The 2017 Uber breach (data: 57M users and drivers) occurred because attackers found credentials in a public GitHub repository — cloud infrastructure credentials stored in a private repo that an employee accidentally made public. The attackers then used those credentials to access Uber's AWS environment, where they found more credentials to Uber's data warehouse. This illustrates the cloud recon principle: credentials cascade. One set of leaked creds leads to another.

**Tools checklist for cloud/mobile recon:**
- [ ] Cloud Enumeration: `bucket-stream`, `s3scanner`, `CloudBrute`, `ScoutSuite`
- [ ] APK Analysis: `jadx`, `apktool`, `MobSF` (Mobile Security Framework)
- [ ] iOS Analysis: `class-dump`, `Hopper`, `objection`
- [ ] GitHub Dorking: `truffleHog`, `gitLeaks`, `git-secrets`
- [ ] Cloud Metadata: `cloud_enum` (multicloud bucket enumeration)
